Hazmat & Chemicals.clue: This is where Safety Data Sheets (SDS) should be kept for shop chemicals.
choices: Locked in the supervisor's office
In a location accessible to all shop
Filed with the manufacturer only
Discarded after opening the chemical
Hazmat & Chemicals.explanation: SDS must be accessible to all personnel at all times, per OSHA. Locked or filed-offsite storage defeats the purpose. Discarding them violates the regulation.

Hazmat & Chemicals.clue: When handling solvents and chemicals, this is the most important first step for a new technician.
choices: Smell the chemical to identify it
Read the Safety Data Sheet (SDS) to understand hazards
Apply the chemical quickly to see how it works
Mix chemicals to see which is stronger
Hazmat & Chemicals.explanation: Reading the SDS is the required first step. Smelling is dangerous (some chemicals are toxic at low exposure). Applying without precaution invites injury. Mixing chemicals can produce violent reactions.

Hazmat & Chemicals.clue: This is the most correct response when a technician feels dizzy from a strong solvent smell while cleaning boards.
choices: Continue working and ignore the symptoms
Leave the area immediately for fresh air
Open the chemical container wider for better access
Sit down next to the chemical and rest
Hazmat & Chemicals.explanation: Leave for fresh air immediately. Continuing work or resting near the source both worsen exposure. Opening the chemical wider increases exposure.

Hazmat & Chemicals.clue: In a GA repair environment, proper disposal of hazardous materials typically involves this.
choices: Pouring them down the shop drain
Placing them in the regular trash
Burning them in a fire barrel
Collecting them in designated labeled
Hazmat & Chemicals.explanation: Proper hazmat disposal uses designated containers and certified disposal services. Drain, trash, and burning are all environmentally illegal and hazardous to personnel.

Hazmat & Chemicals.clue: On an NFPA 704 hazard diamond, this color-coded quadrant indicates a flammability hazard.
choices: Blue
Yellow
Red
White
Hazmat & Chemicals.explanation: NFPA 704 diamond color code: Blue = health hazard, Red = flammability, Yellow = reactivity, White = special hazards (oxidizer, water-reactive, etc.). The number in each quadrant (0-4) indicates severity, with 4 being the most severe.

Fire & Electrical Emergency.clue: Class C fires are commonly associated with this.
choices: Energized electrical equipment such as motors
Ordinary combustibles like wood and paper
Flammable liquids like gasoline and solvents
Cooking oils and greases
Fire & Electrical Emergency.explanation: Class A is ordinary combustibles; Class B is flammable liquids; Class C is energized electrical; Class K is cooking oils.

Fire & Electrical Emergency.clue: When checking a fire extinguisher's readiness in a GA avionics shop, a technician should verify these items.
choices: Only the color of the extinguisher body
Pressure gauge, safety pin, and inspection tag
Only the tag date
Only that the extinguisher exists in the shop
Fire & Electrical Emergency.explanation: A proper readiness check verifies the pressure gauge is in the green zone, the safety pin is intact, and the inspection tag is current. Surface-only or presence-only checks miss common failure modes — low pressure, missing pin, physical damage, expired inspection.

Fire & Electrical Emergency.clue: When a small screw is dropped behind a panel during avionics installation, this is what should be done immediately.
choices: Continue the installation and retrieve the screw later if convenient
Replace the screw with a new one without retrieving the old one
Stop work, locate and retrieve the screw before continuing
Assume the screw will remain harmless and ignore it
Fire & Electrical Emergency.explanation: Immediate retrieval is required. Delaying or ignoring creates a FOD hazard that can cause in-flight shorts, control jams, or equipment damage. Simply replacing the screw does not remove the dropped one.

Fire & Electrical Emergency.clue: This is the immediate, correct first aid response when encountering an electrical shock victim.
choices: Immediately grab the victim to pull them away
Pour water on the victim to cool them
FIRST ensure the power is off
Ignore the situation and continue working
Fire & Electrical Emergency.explanation: Cut power FIRST. Touching a still-energized victim can kill the rescuer. Then call 911 and provide CPR/AED as needed. Water could conduct electricity if the source is still live; ignoring is never acceptable.

Fire & Electrical Emergency.clue: You detect a burning insulation odor in an avionics rack. This is your first action.
choices: Continue working and see if the odor goes away
De-energize the affected circuit(s)
Spray water on the avionics rack
Leave the aircraft and hope the problem resolves
Fire & Electrical Emergency.explanation: De-energize first. Continuing, ignoring, or spraying water on live electronics are all unsafe. Once de-energized, the source can be investigated safely.

Shop Culture & Protection.clue: This practice best prevents ESD damage to sensitive avionics.
choices: Wearing wool clothing to add static comfort on the bench
Walking across carpet before handling electronic components
Handling components with bare hands only while on a stool
Using a grounded wrist strap, ESD mat, and antistatic packaging
Shop Culture & Protection.explanation: Grounded wrist strap, grounded ESD mat, and antistatic packaging together protect against ESD. Wool and carpet generate static. Bare hands alone without grounding offer no protection.

Shop Culture & Protection.clue: This is the most effective way to prevent FOD in an avionics shop.
choices: A layered approach
A once-a-year deep clean
Relying on individual technicians' diligence alone
Installing more trash cans
Shop Culture & Protection.explanation: FOD prevention is systemic, not individual. Layered defenses (accountability, discipline, tracking, awareness) catch FOD incidents before they reach the aircraft. Once-a-year cleaning is too infrequent. Individual diligence alone misses some incidents.

Shop Culture & Protection.clue: When working on aircraft systems that emit RF signals, this is one of the biggest safety concerns.
choices: The aircraft might run out of fuel
Personnel exposure to potentially harmful RF energy
The aircraft paint might fade
The RF signals disturbing nearby Wi-Fi networks
Shop Culture & Protection.explanation: Personnel RF exposure is a real health and safety concern — eyes and reproductive organs are particularly vulnerable. Fuel, paint, and Wi-Fi concerns are not primary shop-safety issues with RF systems.

Shop Culture & Protection.clue: This best describes a 'near-miss' in the context of shop safety.
choices: An unplanned event that did not result in injury
Any minor injury that required first aid
A customer complaint about service
A tool that was temporarily misplaced
Shop Culture & Protection.explanation: A near-miss is a no-consequence warning event that had the potential for bad consequences. It is distinct from actual minor injuries and unrelated to customer service or tool misplacement.

Shop Culture & Protection.clue: This describes a safe practice around moving rotorcraft.
choices: Walk under the rotor disc regardless of rotor speed
Approach from directly behind
Run quickly through the rotor disc area
Maintain eye contact with the pilot, stay in designated safe
Shop Culture & Protection.explanation: Eye contact, safe zones, correct approach direction, staying low, and avoiding the tail rotor are all established safe-approach practices. Walking under, approaching from behind, or running through the disc area are all dangerous.

Extended Coverage.clue: This type of cabinet is required for flammable-chemical storage at an avionics maintenance facility.
choices: An open shelf on the workbench right next to the soldering iron
A stack of cardboard boxes arranged near the building's exit door
A converted household refrigerator repurposed for chemical storage
A UL-listed flammable cabinet, grounded, vented, closed, and labeled
Extended Coverage.explanation: The standard is a UL-listed or FM-approved flammable-liquid cabinet: grounded, vented per local code, self-closing and kept closed, and externally labeled. Open-bench storage near ignition sources, converted household refrigerators (their relays arc), and cardboard storage near exits are all non-compliant and dangerous.
Extended Coverage.targetCaetStem: Scenario: You are tasked with installing a new avionics system that requires grounding. What is the first step to ensure proper grounding?

Extended Coverage.clue: A technician begins a new panel install that calls for a chassis bond. This is the initial action to establish correct grounding.
choices: Apply power and measure case voltage against the frame
Bond the pilot's seat to the airframe with a ground strap
Create an equipotential reference per the install manual
Tie the antenna coax shield to the headset jack ground
Extended Coverage.explanation: Bonding must be established before applying power. Equipotential bonding ties the chassis, the airframe, and any bench test gear to one low-impedance point per the installation manual and AC 43.13-1B Chapter 11. Only then is it safe to energize the system.
Extended Coverage.targetCaetStem: There is a hydraulic fluid spill near the avionics bench Recommended action?

Extended Coverage.clue: A puddle of hydraulic fluid has leaked onto the floor next to an avionics workstation. This is the correct response.
choices: Keep working and wipe the puddle up at the end of the shift
Push the fluid into the closest floor drain with a shop broom
Pour thinner over the puddle to dilute it before walking away
Halt work, warn coworkers, absorb per SDS, and dispose as hazmat
Extended Coverage.explanation: Hydraulic fluid is a slip hazard and a combustible liquid. Halt work and warn coworkers, remove nearby ignition sources, absorb the pool with an approved absorbent pad or granule, clean per the SDS, and place the contaminated absorbent into a labeled hazmat container. Drain disposal violates the Clean Water Act; adding solvent worsens the fire risk.
Extended Coverage.targetCaetStem: How should flammable liquids be stored in an avionics shop?

Extended Coverage.clue: A technician needs product-specific hazard details for a cleaning solvent on the bench. This is the correct reference to consult.
choices: A general internet search on the product name
The Safety Data Sheet for the specific product
The original shipping invoice for the chemical
A handwritten note taped to the storage shelf
Extended Coverage.explanation: The SDS is the authoritative, OSHA-required source, provided by the manufacturer and kept accessible in the shop per OSHA's Hazard Communication Standard. It follows a standardized 16-section format covering hazards, PPE, first aid, handling, spill response, and disposal. Internet results, invoices, and handwritten notes are not compliant and can be wrong.
Extended Coverage.targetCaetStem: Where should you find specific safety info about a chemical solvent used in avionics cleaning?

Extended Coverage.clue: At a Part 145 repair station, this paperwork is required on hand for every chemical product in use.
choices: The chemical product's annual environmental compliance report
A signed purchase order showing the chemical was bought
The Safety Data Sheet (SDS) required by OSHA HazCom standard
The receiving inspection tag from initial container delivery
Extended Coverage.explanation: The SDS (formerly MSDS) is the OSHA-required hazmat document under 29 CFR 1910.1200 — readily available to every employee during every work shift. Environmental reports, purchase orders, and receiving tags are administrative records and do not satisfy the HazCom Standard.
Extended Coverage.targetCaetStem: Which document must be readily available for each hazardous material used in a Part 145 shop?

Extended Coverage.clue: On the ramp and during engine runs, hearing protection is mandatory for good reason. This is the primary hazard it guards against.
choices: Loud noise slows down overall shop productivity
Loud noise interferes with shop radio communication
Sustained ramp noise causes permanent hearing loss
Loud noise makes instrument displays harder to read
Extended Coverage.explanation: The core reason is permanent hearing-loss risk above an 85 dBA 8-hour time-weighted exposure. Sustained noise from engine runs, APUs, GPUs, and pneumatic tools routinely exceeds OSHA's 85 dBA action level and causes permanent, cumulative, irreversible hearing loss. OSHA 29 CFR 1910.95 requires hearing protection, annual audiograms, and a hearing conservation program at that threshold. Productivity, comm clarity, and display readability are secondary.
Extended Coverage.targetCaetStem: In a busy shop with multiple aircraft, why is hearing protection so critical for technicians?

final.clue: Skydrol (phosphate ester hydraulic fluid), often encountered in large aircraft bays, requires immediate cleaning with soap and water upon skin contact because it is highly corrosive and causes what primary physical symptom?
choices: Instant freezing of tissue
Intense burning sensation
Painless numbness
Immediate blistering
final.explanation: Skydrol is notorious for causing intense, painful burning sensations upon contact with skin and, particularly, the eyes. Immediate flushing with water is the required first aid.

final.clue: Before approaching the radome of a powered-up transport category aircraft on the ramp, you must verify what system is pinned or disabled to prevent potentially lethal RF exposure?
choices: The Weather Radar
The TCAS transmitters
The VHF Comm antennas
The GPS receiver
final.explanation: Weather radar systems emit high-power non-ionizing radiation that can cause severe internal burns. They must be off or disabled via pin before personnel work near the nose of the aircraft.

final.clue: When working on highly sensitive avionics LRUs, an ESD wrist strap must be worn. What internal component is built into the wrist strap wire to protect the technician from electrocution if they touch a live power source?
choices: A 1 Amp fuse
A 1 Megohm resistor
A grounding busbar
A diode
final.explanation: A 1MΩ resistor is built into the strap. It allows static electricity to safely bleed to ground, but limits the current to a harmless level (milliamps) if the technician touches 115V AC.
